items.q: What is the doctrine of the Trinity?
items.correct: God is one being existing in three persons (Father, Son, Holy Spirit) — distinct in person but united in essence; Christians remain monotheistic.
items.misconception: Christians believe in three different gods.
items.random_wrong: The Trinity refers to heaven, earth and hell.
items.why: Students treat 'three persons' as 'three gods'. The orthodox position is one God in three distinct persons sharing one substance — a notoriously difficult concept students often misrepresent as polytheism.

items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.q: What is the problem of evil?
items.correct: If God is all-loving, all-knowing and all-powerful, why does evil and suffering exist? Logically, all four cannot be true together without explanation.
items.misconception: It is the question of whether evil people will go to hell.
items.random_wrong: It is the question of whether the devil really exists.
items.why: Students treat it as a moral or eschatological question. It is actually a logical challenge to classical theism — and the central problem theodicies try to solve.

items.q: Is modalism an acceptable way to explain the Trinity?
items.correct: No — modalism (one person wearing three successive masks: Father, then Son, then Spirit) was condemned as heresy; orthodoxy holds the three persons are eternally distinct yet one in substance.
items.misconception: The Trinity means one God who simply plays three different roles at different times, like one actor with three masks.
items.random_wrong: The Trinity means God, Mary and Jesus.
items.why: The 'masks' analogy is precisely the Sabellian error: it cannot account for the Son praying to the Father. Students reach for it as a simplification and unknowingly state a condemned position.

items.q: Did the Council of Nicaea decide which books would be in the Bible?
items.correct: No — Nicaea (325) addressed the Arian controversy and affirmed the Son as homoousios with the Father; the biblical canon developed separately over a longer period.
items.misconception: At Nicaea the bishops voted on which gospels to include in the Bible and suppressed the rest.
items.random_wrong: Nicaea was the council at which Luther posted his 95 theses.
items.why: The canon-at-Nicaea story is a popular myth (amplified by fiction like The Da Vinci Code). Surviving records of Nicaea concern Arianism, the date of Easter and church order — not the canon.

items.q: Which council first defined Christ as 'in two natures, without confusion, without change'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Chalcedon 451. “Nicaea 325” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Of the same substance. “Of a similar substance” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did the Council of Nicaea meet?
items.why: The real figure is 325. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
